title: Redundancy

meta.description: Redundancy pay entitlements, genuine redundancy rules, and who doesn't get redundancy pay.

content:
Redundancy occurs when an employer no longer requires the job done by an employee to be done by anyone, or the employer becomes insolvent.

Genuine redundancy:
A redundancy is genuine if:
1. The employer no longer requires the employee's job to be done by anyone
2. The employer has complied with any obligation under an award or enterprise agreement to consult with the employee about the redundancy
3. It was not reasonable for the employer to redeploy the employee within the employer's enterprise or an associated entity

If all three criteria are met, the employee cannot make an unfair dismissal claim. If the redundancy is not genuine, the employee may be able to claim unfair dismissal.

Redundancy pay entitlements (based on continuous service):
- 1 year but less than 2 years: 4 weeks
- 2 years but less than 3 years: 6 weeks
- 3 years but less than 4 years: 7 weeks
- 4 years but less than 5 years: 8 weeks
- 5 years but less than 6 years: 10 weeks
- 6 years but less than 7 years: 11 weeks
- 7 years but less than 8 years: 13 weeks
- 8 years but less than 9 years: 14 weeks
- 9 years but less than 10 years: 16 weeks
- At least 10 years: 12 weeks (reduced from 16 weeks — consistent with long service leave entitlements)

Note: Redundancy pay is in addition to notice of termination and payout of accrued annual leave.

Who doesn't get redundancy pay?
The following employees are generally NOT entitled to redundancy pay:
- Employees of small businesses (fewer than 15 employees)
- Casual employees
- Employees engaged for a specific period, task or season
- Employees dismissed for serious misconduct
- Apprentices
- Trainees engaged only for the training period
- Employees who are transferred to a new employer on transfer of business (in some circumstances)

Transfer of business:
When a business changes ownership, a new employer may have to recognise the employee's service with the old employer when calculating most entitlements. The rules are complex — advice should be sought for specific transfer of business situations.

key_facts.10_year_reduction_note: Redundancy pay reduces from 16 to 12 weeks at 10+ years — consistent with long service leave entitlements
